title: The Myroure of Oure Ladye [through the Sunday service]
summary: CME00040 is not a direct manuscript transcription and does not contain the complete Myroure. The repository XML represents a U-M electronic selection from John Henry Blunt's 1873 EETS edition: the edition title page, Blunt's separate transcript of the first two pages of the Aberdeen manuscript, and printed pp. 1–174 through the Sunday service. Blunt's main edited text was based chiefly on Richard Fawkes's 1530 print, specifically the copy then and now cataloged at Bishop Cosin's Library, Durham, H.II.24; his Aberdeen transcript and consultation are recorded separately. The one historical Robert Tailour codex now survives as two present repository objects, University of Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941. No verifiable public cover-to-cover facsimile of either present manuscript object was found. This record therefore caches the complete public Blunt scan and exact catalog metadata, points to the work portions within broader sources, and records whole-volume reproduction requests without treating catalog records or selected images as manuscript facsimiles.
review_notes: The two present manuscript objects are parts of one historical codex, but they are neither the same current physical object nor interchangeable facsimiles.
Complete-file coverage for the cached Blunt volume is distinguished from the discontinuous CME00040 work portion within that file.

A future manuscript cache should contain the complete provider-exposed physical object and use work_portion locators for the relevant leaves; it should not be reduced to the text-bearing leaves alone.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00040
entities.description: A public electronic selection from Blunt's 1873 edition, ending at the close of the Sunday service on printed p. 174.

entities.label: John Henry Blunt, The Myroure of Oure Ladye (1873)
entities.description: The complete EETS Extra Series 19 edition. Blunt presents the medieval work from the 1530 black-letter print, chiefly using Bishop Cosin's Library H.II.24, and separately prints a transcript of the first two manuscript pages at p. lx.

bibliographic.citation: John Henry Blunt, ed., The Myroure of Oure Ladye: Containing a Devotional Treatise on Divine Service, with a Translation of the Offices Used by the Sisters of the Brigittine Monastery of Sion, at Isleworth, During the Fifteenth and Sixteenth Centuries (London: published for the Early English Text Society by N. Trübner & Co., 1873), EETS Extra Series 19, lx, 378 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Myroure of Oure Ladye
bibliographic.publisher: Published for the Early English Text Society by N. Trübner & Co.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Blunt's 1873 edition

notes: The scan retains Blunt's preliminaries, complete edited text through p. 378, notes, index, covers, and surrounding scan matter.

entities.label: Richard Fawkes, Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady (1530)
entities.description: The early printed edition completed on 4 November 1530 at the request of Abbess Agnes Jordan and general confessor John Fewterer. It is modeled under the schema's nearest edition type and should not be mistaken for a modern scholarly edition. Blunt's principal base copy was Bishop Cosin's Library, Durham, H.II.24; the printer's exemplar has not been established here.

bibliographic.citation: Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady: very necessary for all relygyous persones ([London]: Richard Fawkes, 4 November 1530), STC 17542; ESTC S111821.
bibliographic.title: Here after folowith the boke callyd the myrroure of Oure Lady

notes: Blunt reports that Durham H.II.24 had a complete text but lacked the engraved title leaf and four pages of errata.
No claim is made that the surviving Tailour codex was Fawkes's printer's exemplar.

entities.label: Folger Digital Collections images for STC 17542 copy 3
entities.description: Publicly viewable images linked from the Folger catalog for imperfect copy 3 (HH145/18a). The old generic catalog wording does not establish whether the image set is cover-to-cover, so reproduction coverage remains unknown.

entities.label: University of Aberdeen MS 134
alternate_labels: Aberdeen University Library W. P. R. 4.18
entities.description: The present Aberdeen first half of the late-fifteenth-century paper Myroure codex copied by Robert Tailour for or associated with Sister Elizabeth Monton. The historical codex's second present part is Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941.

holding.notes: Held by University of Aberdeen Special Collections, Aberdeen, Scotland.

physical_description.extent: One volume; current foliation not supplied in the public catalog
physical_description.format: Codex fragment: first present half of the historical Tailour codex
notes: Blunt's 1873 description calls it a paper quarto of approximately 8 by 6 inches with 270 written pages; that legacy pagination is not substituted for current foliation.
The catalog says access status Open.
The codex ends after the Sunday service; Tailour's colophon calls this sufficient for this half of the Myroure.

entities.label: University of Aberdeen CALM record for MS 134
Bodleian Library MS Rawl. C. 941
entities.description: The present Bodleian second part of the historical Tailour Myroure codex, in Middle English on paper, with Syon provenance, Elizabeth Monton's initials, Tailour's concluding inscription, parchment section tags, and a blind-stamped binding of about 1530.

holding.notes: Held by the Bodleian Libraries, Oxford, England.

physical_description.extent: One present volume; exact leaf and image inventory should be confirmed with the repository
physical_description.format: Codex fragment: second present half of the historical Tailour codex
notes: Blind-stamped binding, circa 1530.
The current catalog explicitly identifies part 1 as Aberdeen MS 134.
It is not the direct base for the CME selection established here; it completes the surviving manuscript lineage of the whole work.

notes: Scribe of the split manuscript, not identified here as author of the Myroure.

Ask for exact current foliation and separate written terms for private retention, public redistribution, scholarly publication, and commercial reuse.

access.format: Web page images; exact inventory unknown
notes: The generic legacy link label does not prove cover-to-cover coverage; no local bundle was created because the exact inventory and rights could not be audited without bypassing provider controls.
Folger copy 3 itself is imperfect, lacking all after R6, so even complete photography of that copy would not reproduce the complete 1530 edition.

rights.basis: Published in 1530, centuries before modern U.S. copyright terms; modern photographs and provider terms are separate.

notes: Public download availability is not treated as a provider copyright dedication.

rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and any jurisdiction specified in supplied terms

rights.component: Folger Digital Collections photographs and presentation
rights.jurisdiction: United States and any jurisdiction specified by provider terms

The exact institution-maintained GitHub repository exposed no explicit license at the checked revision; local caching is recorded as research metadata, not permission to redistribute.
notes: The same conservative no-inferred-license treatment applies to the cached Aberdeen RDF and Folger MARCXML unless their providers supply more specific terms.

editorial_practices.description: The electronic text combines the edition title page, Blunt's p. lx manuscript sample, and printed pp. 1–174, then stops at the Tailour/Sunday boundary instead of continuing through Blunt's complete edition.

Blunt chiefly follows the 1530 Fawkes print represented by Bishop Cosin's Library H.II.24, while separately consulting and describing the Aberdeen manuscript and reproducing only its first two pages as a transcript at p. lx.
editorial_practices.scope: Complete 1873 edition; direct Aberdeen transcript limited to legacy manuscript pages 1–2

evidence.citation: Local namespace-aware XML inspection and comparison with Blunt's pagination for CME00040.
evidence.locator: BODY/DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, PB, MILESTONE, P, NOTE, and final CLOSER/SIGNED
evidence.summary: Established 8 DIV1, 90 DIV2, 176 distinct PB references, 127 milestones, 111 P, 845 NOTE, and exact coverage of the edition title page, p. lx, and pp. 1–174. The XML ends after Sunday with Tailour's half-volume colophon and does not encode Monday–Saturday or later matter.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, bibliographic record 164108, STC 17542.
evidence.summary: Current record for Fawkes's 4 November 1530 edition, STC 17542/ESTC S111821, including extent, three Folger copies, copy 3's incompleteness, and links to digital and binding images.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library MARCXML export for record 164108.
evidence.summary: Cached machine-readable bibliographic metadata for the 1530 print and Folger holdings; it is not a facsimile.

evidence.summary: Public image links for Folger copy 3; the exact image inventory and completeness were not available for automated audit without provider human verification.

evidence.citation: Folger cataloging policy, MARC 856 Electronic Location and Access.
evidence.summary: Current cataloging guidance distinguishes 'Cover-to-cover images' from 'Selected image(s)' and notes that older generic link labels may be less specific, so the generic exact-record link cannot establish complete coverage.

evidence.citation: Dated audit attempt for a complete Folger image inventory of STC 17542 copy 3.
evidence.summary: The public landing page was reachable, but inventory enumeration required provider human verification; no control was bypassed and no cover-to-cover claim was made.

evidence.citation: Folger Digital Collections reuse guidance.
evidence.summary: Requests attribution and directs users to photo@folger.edu for a formal permission letter when a publisher requires one; no standardized exact-item image license was found.

evidence.citation: Alexandra Verini, 'Sisters and Friends: The Medieval Nuns of Syon Abbey,' in Women's Friendship in Medieval Literature (Ohio State University Press, 2022), chapter 4.
evidence.summary: Recent scholarship describes the fifteenth-century Middle English translation and explication of the Bridgettine office, discusses possible Thomas Fishbourne authorship, and identifies the Tailour manuscript as split between Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian Rawl. C. 941.
notes: The open-book page was identified, but scripted PDF retrieval met the provider's human-verification barrier; no local copy was cached.

evidence.citation: Local source-cache checksum, size, coverage, and provenance audit for CME00040.
evidence.summary: Verified the complete Blunt PDF, Internet Archive metadata and page map, Aberdeen RDF catalog metadata, Bodleian catalog TEI, and Folger MARCXML against the local-copy records. No manuscript image bundle was cached.

open_questions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of both present parts of the historical Tailour codex—University of Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941—be obtained, retained locally, and mapped leaf by leaf?
next_steps: Ask each repository for any complete existing photography, microfilm, or unindexed digital surrogate before commissioning new work.

Request each entire present physical object, including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, every leaf, adjacent material, parchment tags, and edge views if offered—not only the Myroure text or the CME portion.
Obtain exact current foliation and map Blunt's legacy manuscript pages 1–2 plus the CME text boundary within the complete deliveries.
Confirm private retention, Git redistribution, scholarly publication, print-on-demand publication, and commercial use separately in writing.

open_questions.question: What are the exact current folio ranges and codicological join between the Myroure portions in Aberdeen MS 134 and Bodleian MS Rawl. C. 941?
next_steps: Request current foliation and complete image inventories from both repositories.
Crosswalk Blunt's 270-page legacy Aberdeen description and pages 1–2 sample to current folios.
Record first and final work leaves, any non-Myroure matter, the original split, and current binding boundaries without inferring them from printed pagination.

open_questions.question: Which anonymous Syon brother composed, translated, and commented on the Myroure, and how narrowly can its composition be dated?
next_steps: Review Ann Hutchison's textual scholarship and the evidence for Thomas Fishbourne, Clement Maydeston, Simon Winter, and other proposed Syon authors.
Treat Blunt's Thomas Gascoigne proposal as a historical disputed attribution rather than established authorship.
Keep the conservative post-1415 to mid-fifteenth-century range until a source-based reassessment is complete.

open_questions.question: What manuscript or textual exemplar did Fawkes use in 1530, and is there a verifiably complete public or licensable facsimile of a complete surviving copy?
next_steps: Do not infer that Fawkes used the surviving Tailour codex.
Audit complete surviving copies, including Durham H.II.24 and Cambridge A.B.10.41, against current catalogs and image services.
Ask Folger whether the copy 3 image set is cover-to-cover while remembering that copy 3 itself lacks all after R6.
Cache a complete provider-exposed physical copy only when its inventory and retention terms can be verified, and then map the CME edition portion within it.

open_questions.question: Why does CME00040 stop at the Aberdeen/Sunday half-volume boundary, and what initial keying, image, correction, and reuse history precedes the documented 2016 review?
next_steps: Request item-level production metadata and any source image package from U-M.
Confirm whether the manuscript-boundary selection was intentional and whether earlier CME phases preserve additional documentation.
Treat TEMPHEAD as evidence of 2016 validation and proofing rather than initial creation.

open_questions.question: What exact manuscript or early-print features underlie every italicized expansion and other editorial intervention represented in CME00040?
next_steps: Collate Blunt's editorial conventions, the 1530 base print, and the Aberdeen pages 1–2 sample before interpreting italics uniformly.
Use complete-object facsimiles and exact work-portion mappings when they become available.
Distinguish Blunt's intervention from U-M XML rendition and repository PDF styling.
